# Mini-corpus (differential-testing corpus for the Rust backend port)

A curated, deterministic corpus of real frontend CFGs (pre-optimization, exactly the
artifact `rust/ENCODING.md` round-trips) captured from the behavioral test suite,
plus behavioral I/O vectors for replaying callback runs. It is the shared test data
for the encoding round-trip checks (T0.4/T0.5) and the later differential-testing
stages (T1.2, T2.3, T2.4). Everything in `cfgs/`, `dumps/`, `vectors/` and
`manifest.json` is generated by `tools/gen_corpus.py` — do not edit by hand.

## Layout

```
manifest.json            counts, sizes, per-entry provenance + features (schema below)
cfgs/<sha256>.scfg       encoded CFG bytes (rust/ENCODING.md v1); <sha256> = hash of the bytes
dumps/<sha256>.txt       Python-side canonical structural dump of the same CFG
vectors/<sha256>.json    behavioral I/O vectors for that CFG (only for CFGs that were
                         actually executed by the legacy interpreter during capture)
```

These files are marked `-text` in `.gitattributes`: comparisons are byte-exact and
must never be EOL-converted.

## Why the dump is stored

Round-trip validation compares canonical structural dumps (ENCODING.md §5) that both
sides produce independently: Python renders it from the live CFG objects
(`sonolus.backend.encode.cfg_canonical_dump`) at capture time, Rust from the decoded
bytes (`sonolus_backend_core::cfg::canonical_dump`). Python cannot re-derive its dump
from the encoded bytes alone, so the Python-side dump is stored here; both the Rust
test (`sonolus-backend-core/tests/corpus.rs`) and the Python test
(`tests/backend/test_corpus_roundtrip.py`) compare their side's dump against this
single stored artifact.

## I/O vector schema (v1)

Captured in `tests/script/conftest.py` when the legacy Python interpreter runs a
compiled callback (see `tests/corpus_capture.py`). One JSON file per CFG:

```
{"schema": 1, "cfg": "<sha256>", "vectors": [<vector>, ...]}
```

Each `<vector>` object:

| Field | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `level` | Python optimization level whose compiled artifact produced this run (`minimal`/`fast`/`standard`) |
| `runtime_checks` | runtime-check mode the callback was compiled with (`none`/`terminate`/`notify_and_terminate`) |
| `temp_memory_block` | block id of temporary memory (10000); writes there are slot-allocation-specific and must be ignored when comparing across different pipelines |
| `inputs` | initial block memory before the run: `[[block_id, [value, ...]], ...]`, sorted by block id (block 3000 is EngineRom). All other memory starts empty; reads of unwritten cells yield `-1.0` |
| `rng` | RNG tape, in draw order: `[[kind, lo, hi, value], ...]` with kind `"uniform"` (Op.Random) or `"randrange"` (Op.RandomInteger). Replay by returning `value` for each successive draw |
| `result` | value returned by running the root node |
| `log` | DebugLog entries, in order |
| `writes` | final value of every cell the program wrote (last-write-wins): `[[block_id, index, value], ...]`, sorted. Includes the test-harness cells: block -1 index 0 is set to 1 when the callback ran to completion (i.e. was not terminated by a runtime check), block -2 holds the result value(s) |

Values are JSON numbers when finite (bit-exact round-trip) and raw IEEE-754 bit
strings (`"0x%016x"`) for NaN/±inf, preserving NaN payloads.

A vector replays exactly against the artifact semantics it was captured from
(`level`); a semantics-preserving pipeline at any level must reproduce `result`,
`log`, and all `writes` outside `temp_memory_block`, provided RNG draws are fed from
the tape in order. Vectors are captured only from non-hypothesis tests.

## Manifest schema (v1)

Top level: `schema`, `encoding_version`, `count`, `vector_total`, `cfg_bytes`,
`dump_bytes`, `vector_bytes`, `total_bytes` (≤ 5,000,000 budget, enforced by the
Rust corpus test), `capture_stats` (unique CFG count of the source capture run,
hypothesis-only exclusions, encode rejects with verbatim reasons) and `entries`,
sorted by hash:

- `hash`, `cfg_size`, `dump_size`, `vector_size`, `vectors` (vector count)
- `sources`: up to 3 source test ids
- `callbacks`: up to 3 `mode/callbackName` pairs (real engine callbacks only)
- `features`: `blocks`, `stmts`, `max_edges`, `depth`, `unique_ops` plus booleans
  `loop`, `switch`, `dynamic_index`, `nested_block`, `float_cond`, `rng`

## Regenerating

```
rm -rf .corpus-capture
SONOLUS_CAPTURE_CORPUS=.corpus-capture uv run pytest tests -n 32
uv run python tools/gen_corpus.py .corpus-capture
```

(Requires the extension: `uv run maturin develop -m rust/sonolus-backend-py/Cargo.toml`.
`.corpus-capture/` is gitignored; capture output must never be committed.)

The tool is deterministic: the same capture directory always produces a
byte-identical corpus. Selection is diversity-first within the ~5MB budget:
hypothesis-derived CFGs are excluded entirely; then (1) the biggest real callbacks,
(2) every `(mode, callback)` pair and mode observed, (3) feature buckets (loops,
switches, dynamic indexing, nested block derefs, float edge conds, RNG use, deepest
expressions, vector-bearing entries), (4) a hash-ordered fill to the budget. Very
large callbacks beyond the per-entry caps (e.g. the pydori tutorial `update`
callback, ~2.5MB cfg+dump) are deliberately excluded to preserve diversity.
